payload.title: Spike: realtime fan-out across multiple app servers
payload.description:
OPEN QUESTION: How does a mutation accepted on app-server A reach an SSE/WS subscriber connected to app-server B?

Today: an in-process EventEmitter bus ([[21]]) drives SSE; a single process sees every mutation. The moment we run >1 app server behind a load balancer this breaks — the single biggest 'it's not local anymore' change.

Questions to resolve:
- External pub/sub choice: Redis pub/sub, NATS, Postgres LISTEN/NOTIFY, a managed bus? Tradeoffs for our scale + ops burden.
- Keep SSE (unidirectional, simple, what we ship today) or move to WebSocket (bidirectional — needed if push uses the same channel)? SSE-over-WAN reconnect behavior is a known unknown — test it.
- Per-tenant / per-workspace topic routing so a subscriber only gets its own events.
- Connection scale: how many concurrent SSE/WS connections per node, and how the LB handles long-lived connections (sticky? keepalive tuning?).
- What happens to the existing hub watchdog / Bonjour discovery / port-race self-healing ([[49]]) — it becomes LB + failover + health checks; document what we drop.

Deliverable: comment with a proposed fan-out architecture. Likely spawns: 'External pub/sub bus integration', 'WAN SSE reconnect hardening', and possibly 'WebSocket transport' if we decide to switch.
